title: Apple sauce
subtitle: Bramleys cooked down with butter — the roast pork companion
excerpt: A pure, tart apple sauce from Bramley apples: cooked to a soft, rough purée with butter and a touch of sugar. Fifteen minutes from start to the bowl, and the right thing alongside roast pork, pork chops, or sausages.
sourceNotes: Classic British sauce tradition documented in Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — 'Apple Sauce for Geese, Pork, etc.' Bramley Seedling became the standard British cooking apple after the 1860s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months in small containers. Defrost overnight in the fridge.
recipe.batchNotes: Scales well. A batch of six apples produces enough for two or three roasts and freezes easily.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be made 3 days ahead and kept covered in the fridge. Serve warm or at room temperature.

content.text: Bramley apples collapse into a soft purée almost the moment they hit heat, which is exactly what you want for apple sauce. The variety is tart enough to cut through the fat of roast pork without needing more than a small amount of sugar to settle it. Eating apples can be used in an emergency, but they hold their shape rather than dissolving and produce a chunkier, sweeter result.

The method is minimal: peel, chop, cook with a small amount of water and butter until soft, then stir or beat to a purée as rough or smooth as you like. Some versions add a strip of lemon peel while it cooks; some add a clove. Both are worth trying if you want more complexity.

Peel, core, and roughly chop {{apple-bramley}} Bramley apples into 2 cm pieces. Place in a small saucepan with {{water}} of water and {{unsalted-butter}} of unsalted butter. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the apples have completely collapsed.

Remove from the heat and stir in {{granulated-sugar}} of granulated sugar. Beat with a wooden spoon for a rough purée, or press through a sieve if you prefer a smoother finish. Taste and add more sugar or the juice of the lemon if using. The sauce should be tart enough to stand up to fatty pork; err on the side of sharp.

items.cause: The apple variety was sweeter than expected, or too much sugar was added
items.fix: Squeeze in a little lemon juice and stir. The sharpness returns immediately.

items.symptom: Sauce is too thin and watery
items.cause: Apples had a high water content, or too much water was added
items.fix: Return to the heat uncovered for 3 to 5 minutes, stirring, to evaporate the excess liquid.

items.symptom: Eating apples produced chunks rather than purée
items.cause: Eating apple varieties hold their cell structure under heat, unlike Bramleys
items.fix: Press through a sieve or blitz briefly with a stick blender.

items.cause: Cooked at too high a heat, or left uncovered for too long
items.fix: Discard and start again. Browned apple sauce tastes cooked-out and flat.

content.text: Apple sauce with roast pork is a pairing documented in English cookery since at least the seventeenth century, long before the Bramley seedling was propagated in Southwell, Nottinghamshire, in the 1860s. Before Bramleys, cooking apples of various tartness served the same function. The acid of the apple cuts the fat of the pork in a way that no herb or spice quite replicates, which is why the combination has persisted unchanged for so long. It goes as well with pork chops and sausages as with a whole roast.
